message.text: User-supplied doc_id is interpolated directly into SQL query via f-string (f"SELECT file_path FROM documents WHERE id = {doc_id}"). No parameterized query or input validation is used. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL to extract file_path values pointing to sensitive files, manipulate query logic via UNION-based injection, or exfiltrate database contents. This SQLi is the first stage of a chained attack that feeds attacker-controlled file paths into the subsequent open() call.

The file_path value retrieved from the SQL query is passed directly to open(file_path, 'r') without any path validation, canonicalization, or allowlist check. When chained with the preceding SQL injection, an attacker can inject a UNION SELECT to return an arbitrary file path (e.g., '/etc/shadow', '/proc/self/environ', application source code), which is then read and returned in the HTTP response. Even without SQLi, if file_path values in the database are attacker-influenced, this constitutes a local file inclusion vulnerability.

The database model method that retrieves document metadata constructs SQL queries using string interpolation with the doc_id parameter rather than parameterized queries. This is the underlying data access layer vulnerability that enables the SQL injection at the endpoint level.
